tier: Semantic vs. Instance vs. Panoptic Segmentation
title: Instance Segmentation
concept: Instance segmentation detects and segments individual objects of interest, giving each distinct object its own unique pixel mask.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main limitation of semantic segmentation?

options.text: It cannot distinguish between individual objects of the same class
It is only capable of binary (yes/no) output

exercises.explain: Correct! Semantic segmentation merges all objects of the same class into one mask.

cards.title: Count the cars
cards.body: Imagine you are building a parking monitoring system. If you use semantic segmentation, all cars merge into a single red blob. You can't count them! You need the model to say: 'This is Car 1, that is Car 2, and that is Car 3', and outline each separately.

cards.title: Instance Segmentation
cards.body: Instance Segmentation is a hybrid task. It detects individual objects (like object detection) and segments them (like semantic segmentation), giving each distinct object instance its own unique mask.

cards.title: Naming name tags
cards.body: Imagine a party. Semantic segmentation just colors everyone's clothes blue because they are 'Humans'. Instance segmentation gives everyone a unique name tag: 'John', 'Sarah', and 'David', with their exact boundaries.

cards.title: Fruit sorting robot
cards.body: A robotic arm sorts apples on a conveyor belt. Using instance segmentation, the robot sees 'Apple 1', 'Apple 2', and 'Apple 3' as separate entities, allowing it to grab them one by one.

cards.title: Background is ignored
cards.body: Unlike semantic segmentation which classifies every single pixel (including sky and road), instance segmentation focuses only on detectable objects of interest (called 'things'). It usually ignores the background ('stuff').

cards.title: Semantic vs. Instance
cards.body: Comparing how they paint overlapping objects:

left.text:
All dogs = Blue mask
Overlaps form one big blob
No counting possible

right.text:
Dog 1 = Green, Dog 2 = Red
Clear boundary between them
Enables counting & tracking

exercises.prompt: What does instance segmentation do that semantic segmentation cannot?

options.text: Differentiate between individual objects of the same class
Classify background pixels like sky and grass
Process images faster using fewer layers

exercises.explain: Correct! Instance segmentation distinguishes between individual instances.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Instance segmentation typically segments background classes like the sky and road.
exercises.explain: False. Instance segmentation focuses on individual, countable objects ('things') and ignores background ('stuff').

exercises.sentence: Instance segmentation combines object detection and ___ segmentation.
exercises.explain: It detects objects and then semantically segments them individually.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks requires instance segmentation?

options.text: Tracking and segmenting individual cells on a petri dish
Coloring all pixels representing water in a lake
Deciding if an image shows a healthy leaf or a sick leaf

exercises.explain: Since cells overlap and you need to track them individually, instance segmentation is required.

exercises.prompt: You are building a system for a warehouse robot to count and pick up packages. The packages are stacked together. Why do you need instance segmentation instead of semantic segmentation?

options.text: Semantic segmentation would merge the touching packages into one big mask, whereas instance segmentation identifies and outlines each individual package.
Semantic segmentation cannot run on warehouse cameras.
Instance segmentation is simpler and uses fewer weights.
Packages are too large for semantic segmentation.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Instance segmentation is crucial whenever you need to identify and interact with individual object instances that touch or overlap. Next, let's look at how to combine both worlds!
